hero.tagline: Design 360° wrap art for cups, mugs, and bottles — right in your browser.

custom.name: Name
custom.summary: Bottom Ø {bottomD} mm · top Ø {topD} mm · circumference {cMin}–{cMax} mm · artboard {w} × {h} mm

errors.name: Enter a name.
errors.height: Height must be a positive number of millimeters.
errors.dimensionMissing: Enter a diameter or a circumference.
errors.dimensionBoth: Enter either a diameter or a circumference, not both.
errors.dimensionPositive: Diameter and circumference must be positive numbers.
errors.engraveOrder: The engrave zone top must be above its bottom.
errors.engraveRange: The engrave zone must fit within the vessel height.

viewer.webglUnavailable: WebGL isn't available in this browser, so the 3D preview can't start.

import.empty: No importable shapes found in that SVG.

export.title: Export for your laser

lightburn.name: LightBurn
lightburn.notes: File → Import, then enable the rotary under Tools → Rotary Setup and enter the object diameter.

xtool.name: xTool Creative Space
xtool.notes: Import the SVG into XCS, choose cylindrical (rotary) processing and enter the object diameter.

lasergrbl.name: LaserGRBL
lasergrbl.notes: Open the SVG, enable the rotary axis and set steps-per-rotation from your machine's documentation.

generic.name: Generic SVG
generic.notes: Physical size is true millimeters — import at 100% scale and set up your rotary manually.

export.flattenShapesHint: Converts rects, ellipses and polygons to SVG paths for simpler importers.
export.embedMetadataHint: Adds a comment with the object diameter and setup notes to the file.

warnings.textFontsLightburn: Text stays as live text — LightBurn needs the same font installed, or convert text to paths first.
warnings.textFonts: Text stays as live text — the receiving program needs the same font installed.
warnings.imagesEmbedded: Raster images are embedded as data URIs, which can make the SVG large.
warnings.rasterFonts: Text is rendered with this device's fonts into the PNG.
warnings.transparentBg: Transparent background: some laser programs render transparency as black.

export.rotaryReminder: Rotary reminder: enter object diameter {diameter} mm in your laser software.
export.projectDownloadHint: A .lasergen.json backup you can re-import in laser-gen.

rotary.dpiNote: SVG exports are true millimeters — import at 100% scale. Raster exports embed their DPI.
rotary.step1: Open Tools → Rotary Setup and enable the rotary.
rotary.step2: Chuck rotary: enter steps-per-rotation from your machine docs. Roller rotary: enter your roller diameter.
rotary.step3: Enter the object diameter above as the rotary's object diameter.

vectorize.title: Vectorize
vectorize.noSource: Import an image onto the artboard or upload one to trace.

photo.title: Photo prep
photo.selectHint: Select an image on the artboard — or add one — to prepare it for engraving.
photo.tooManyDots: Too many dots ({count}) — increase the cell size.
photo.resolutionNote: Processing is capped at {px} px on the long edge.

powderCoatedSteel.name: Powder-coated steel
powderCoatedSteel.note: The laser burns the coating away to bright metal, so dark tones stay dark (no invert). Extra contrast and Floyd–Steinberg keep tones crisp.

bareStainless.name: Bare stainless
bareStainless.note: Marking darkens bare steel; a mild contrast boost with Floyd–Steinberg keeps gradients smooth.

wood.name: Wood
wood.note: Wood burns dark and bleeds a little; softer contrast and an ordered 8×8 dither avoid muddy, over-burned patches.

glass.name: Glass
glass.note: Glass etches bright where the beam hits, so the image is inverted; Floyd–Steinberg keeps the frosting detailed.

coatedCeramic.name: Coated ceramic
coatedCeramic.note: Like powder coat, the glaze burns to bright; moderate contrast with Floyd–Steinberg suits coated mugs and tiles.

pack.downloadHint: Self-contained project file — images, an uploaded 3D model and vessel settings travel inside.
pack.confirmReplace: Opening this file replaces the current project. Continue?
pack.openError: Couldn't open that file — it isn't a valid .laserpack project.
pack.importProjectError: That file isn't a valid .laserpack project.

library.importMergePrompt: Merge with your existing library? Press OK to merge, Cancel to replace everything.
library.importError: That file isn't a valid laser-gen library export.
library.emptyHint: Design a wrap in the studio, then save it to your library with the save button in the toolbar.

jobs.title: Job tracker
jobs.notePlaceholder: How did it burn? Settings, issues, results…

assets.title: Assets
assets.emptyHint: Save art here to reuse it across projects.

ai.title: AI assistant
ai.generatingHint: Asking the model for SVG art — this can take a few seconds.

svg.title: Prompt to SVG
svg.placeholder: A leaping fox, geometric line art…
svg.failed: The model did not return usable SVG.

styleHints.mandala: Radially symmetric design centered on the canvas.
styleHints.monogram: Elegant initials drawn as line art.
styleHints.geometric: Repeating pattern that tiles seamlessly around the 360° wrap.
styleHints.halftone-friendly: Bold high-contrast shapes suited to dither/halftone engraving.

image.title: Prompt to image
image.vectorizeHint: The image is on the artboard — run Vectorize on it for engraving-ready paths.
image.hiddenNote: Prompt to image needs an image-capable provider (OpenAI) — the active provider is chat-only.
image.hiddenTooltip: Add an OpenAI provider in Settings to unlock prompt-to-image.

copilot.title: Design copilot
copilot.contextHint: The copilot sees your current project — vessel, artboard size and layers.

examples.example1: Add the text ‘Adventure Awaits’ at 24 mm
examples.example2: Resize the selected art to fit the wrap
examples.example3: Tile this design 4× around the cup

onboarding.intro: The assistant turns ideas into engraving-ready art and can edit the design for you — powered by your own API key.

promptToArt.title: Prompt to art
promptToArt.body: Describe an idea and get an editable vector layer fitted to the wrap — style presets like mandala, monogram and line art.

image.title: Image to engraving
image.body: Generate an image from words, then dither or vectorize it — needs an image-capable provider like OpenAI.

copilot.title: Design copilot
copilot.body: Chat instructions that edit the project: “add ‘Est. 2026’ in 20 mm text”, “tile this pattern around the cup”.

private.title: Private by design
private.body: Keys stay on this device; calls go straight from your browser to the provider.

ai.title: AI providers
ai.label: Name
ai.baseUrlHint: Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint: Ollama, LM Studio, OpenRouter, vLLM… Must allow browser requests (CORS).
ai.errorBaseUrl: A base URL is required for custom endpoints.
ai.errorKey: An API key is required for this provider.
ai.empty: No AI providers configured yet. Add one to enable the studio AI assistant — your key stays on this device.
ai.privacyBody: Keys are stored encrypted on this device only (AES-GCM in IndexedDB) and are never shown again after saving. Requests go directly from your browser to the provider — no proxy, no telemetry. Note: this protects keys at rest, not against a compromised device.

settings.placeholder: App preferences and AI provider keys (stored only on this device) will live here.

meta.title: laser-gen — 360° wrap art for laser engraving
meta.description: Design wrap art for cups, mugs and bottles in your browser: vector editor, live 3D preview, photo-to-engraving, AI assistant, LightBurn/xTool/LaserGRBL export. Free, open source, offline-capable.

hero.eyebrow: Free · Open source · 100% in your browser
hero.sub: A fully client-side PWA for laser engraving: draw, trace, or generate art, preview it live on a 3D vessel, and export millimeter-perfect files for your laser software.

features.title: Everything between photo and burn
features.subtitle: One tool for the whole wrap workflow — no installs, no accounts, no uploads.

wrap360.title: 360° wrap editor
wrap360.body: A millimeter-accurate artboard that maps the full circumference of your vessel, with seam and safe-zone guides.

preview3d.title: Live 3D preview
preview3d.body: See your design wrapped around the vessel in real time, with a laser-sweep shader showing exactly where the beam will travel.

photo.title: Photo to engraving
photo.body: Brightness, contrast and gamma plus Floyd–Steinberg, ordered dither, halftone and stipple — tuned per material.

vectorize.title: Vectorizer
vectorize.body: Trace raster images into clean vector paths right in the browser, with smoothing and simplification controls.

ai.title: AI assistant (BYOK)
ai.body: Prompt-to-vector art fitted to your wrap, prompt-to-image for dithering, and a chat copilot that edits the project — with your own Anthropic, OpenAI or compatible key, which stays on your device.

library.title: Library & jobs
library.body: Save projects and reusable assets locally, track burn attempts with power, speed and passes, and attach result photos.

export.title: Export for your laser
export.body: Physical-size SVG and DPI-correct PNG presets for LightBurn, xTool Creative Space and LaserGRBL, with rotary setup hints.

pick.title: Pick a vessel
pick.body: Choose a preset — Stanley Quencher, camp mug, wine tumbler and more — or measure your own.

design.title: Design or trace
design.body: Draw vectors, import SVGs, prep a photo, or let the AI assistant sketch something for you.

export.title: Export & burn
export.body: Download a millimeter-perfect file, set up your rotary, and watch it engrave.

vessels.title: Made for real vessels
vessels.subtitle: Built-in presets with real-world dimensions — the engrave window highlighted.
vessels.disclaimer: Preset dimensions are approximate community measurements, not manufacturer specs. Measure your own vessel before a production engraving.

openSource.title: Open source, MIT licensed
openSource.body: laser-gen is a community project. No backend, no telemetry, no paywall — audit every line, or help shape the roadmap.

footer.tagline: 360° wrap art for laser engraving, fully in your browser.
footer.mit: MIT licensed · Fully client-side · No telemetry

site.offline: You're offline — laser-gen keeps working; everything is stored on this device.

gettingStarted.intro: From zero to first burn in about five minutes — everything runs locally in your browser.
gettingStarted.quick1: Open the Studio and pick a vessel preset — or measure yours and enter the profile.
gettingStarted.quick2: Design on the unwrapped artboard: draw shapes, import an SVG, prep a photo, or ask the AI assistant.
gettingStarted.quick3: Check the live 3D preview — the wrap, seam and handle safe zones update as you edit.
gettingStarted.quick4: Export for your laser software, enter the object diameter in its rotary setup, and burn.
gettingStarted.wrapBody1: The artboard is the vessel's outer surface unrolled flat: its width equals the circumference at the widest engraved row, its height the engrave window. Anything you draw maps straight onto the vessel.
gettingStarted.wrapBody2: The left and right edges are the same line on the vessel — the seam. Elements that cross an edge wrap automatically, so continuous patterns stay continuous.
gettingStarted.diagramAlt: Diagram: a cylinder with its engrave band, unwrapped into a flat rectangle whose dashed edges mark the seam.
gettingStarted.offlineBody: laser-gen is an installable PWA: the whole app is cached on your device and every project is stored locally in IndexedDB. Design on the shop network — or with no network at all.
gettingStarted.walkTitle: Your first wrap, step by step
gettingStarted.altStudio: The laser-gen studio: wrap artboard on the left, live 3D tumbler preview on the right.
gettingStarted.altExport: The export dialog on the SVG tab, with program presets for LightBurn, xTool Creative Space and LaserGRBL.

walk.step1Body: Open the Studio and choose a preset in the vessel switcher on the right — or click Custom vessel and enter your own measurements. The artboard resizes to that vessel’s engrave window.
walk.step2Body: Draw with the toolbar tools, import an SVG, prep a photo in the photo panel, or ask the AI assistant. Everything you place maps straight onto the vessel’s surface.
walk.step3Body: Watch the live 3D preview: wrap, seam and handle safe zone update as you edit. Drag to rotate and check the full 360°.
walk.step4Body: Click Export and download the SVG with the preset for your laser software. The file carries real millimeter dimensions.
walk.step5Body: In LightBurn’s rotary setup, enter the object diameter from the export dialog’s Rotary tab, frame the job, and burn.

aiProviders.intro: The AI assistant is bring-your-own-key: your browser talks directly to the provider you configure.
aiProviders.whatIntro: Four things it can do, once a provider is configured:
aiProviders.model1: No backend, no proxy — requests go straight from your browser to the provider API.
aiProviders.model2: Keys are encrypted at rest (AES-GCM in IndexedDB) and never shown again after saving.
aiProviders.model3: A key is sent only to its own provider, over HTTPS, when you invoke an AI feature.
aiProviders.model4: Delete a provider in Settings and nothing retains it.
aiProviders.providersBody: One small interface with an adapter per provider; vendor SDKs load lazily, so they only cost bundle size when actually used.
aiProviders.rowOpenai: Chat (default gpt-4o-mini) plus image generation via gpt-image-1.
aiProviders.rowCustom: Ollama, LM Studio, OpenRouter, vLLM — any chat-completions endpoint.
aiProviders.corsBody: Because requests originate in the browser, the endpoint must allow cross-origin browser requests:
aiProviders.corsLocal: Local servers: start Ollama with OLLAMA_ORIGINS set (e.g. OLLAMA_ORIGINS="*" for local use) or your requests will be blocked.
aiProviders.corsHosted: Hosted gateways: OpenRouter and most hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoints send permissive CORS headers; Anthropic and OpenAI's official APIs accept browser requests with your own key.
aiProviders.securityBody: Encryption at rest protects keys on an intact device — it cannot protect against a compromised device. Use scoped keys where possible; see SECURITY.md in the repo for the full threat model.
aiProviders.altSettings: The settings page with the AI providers section and the add-provider form.
aiProviders.altPanel: The AI panel in the studio showing the onboarding capability showcase.

body.setup1: Open Settings → AI providers → Add provider. Every entry needs a label and a model id; the rest depends on the kind:
body.setupAnthropic: Anthropic — create a key at console.anthropic.com and paste it; the default chat model is claude-sonnet-4-5.
body.setupOpenai: OpenAI — create a key at platform.openai.com. Also unlocks prompt-to-image via gpt-image-1.
body.setupCustom: Custom — any OpenAI-compatible chat-completions endpoint: Ollama (http://localhost:11434/v1), LM Studio, OpenRouter, vLLM, corporate gateways. Needs a base URL and model id; the key is optional if the endpoint doesn’t require one.
body.setupTest: Then hit Test connection: a green dot means the browser really reached the endpoint, and failures name the cause — bad key, network, or CORS.
body.featSvg: Prompt → SVG: describe line art (“mountain range band, 20 mm tall”) and it lands as a sanitized vector layer, fitted to your artboard.
body.featImage: Prompt → image (OpenAI only): generates a raster onto the artboard, one click away from the Vectorize panel.
body.featCopilot: Copilot: a chat that knows your current document and can run a small fixed command set — addText, resizeToFit, tile360 — each action reported line by line, never executed silently.
body.featSave: Every generation can be saved to the library as an asset.

aiProviders.altOnboarding: The AI panel’s onboarding view with four capability cards: prompt to art, image to engraving, design copilot and privacy.

vessels.intro: Presets spare you the calipers: pick a vessel and its profile drives both the 3D preview and the artboard.
vessels.measureBody: Presets are approximate community measurements, not manufacturer specs. For production work, measure height and diameter at a few points with a ruler or calipers — wall taper changes the circumference your art wraps across.
vessels.contributeBody: Measured a vessel we're missing? Presets are small profile definitions — see
vessels.modelCreditsBody: The camp mug and classic ceramic mug presets render community 3D models from Sketchfab, licensed CC-BY-4.0: “Stanley Mug” by Lime Zigubre and “Plain Mug” by LightSwitch. The same attribution appears next to the 3D preview when one of these vessels is active, and in the repository's NOTICE.md. All other vessels are parametric lathe profiles rendered by laser-gen itself.
vessels.altSwitcher: The vessel switcher with preset and custom vessels.
vessels.altCustom: The custom vessel dialog with diameter, height and engrave-window fields filled in.
vessels.altColor: The studio with a custom powder-coat color applied to the tumbler preview.

body.custom1: Measure your vessel and click Custom vessel in the switcher. Enter diameter or circumference — circumference is often easier: wrap a tape measure around the cup and the app divides by π for you.
body.custom2: Tapered vessels (most tumblers) need the diameter at both the bottom and the top of the engrave window; straight cylinders need just one. Set the engrave window to the band you can actually burn — below the lip, above the base step.
body.custom3: Custom vessels persist on this device and behave exactly like presets: they drive the artboard size, the 3D preview and the rotary numbers in the export dialog.
body.color: The finish selector under the 3D preview switches between powder-coat colors and bare stainless; Custom powder-coat unlocks the color picker, which tints the vessel and the wrap texture to match your real cup. The choice is cosmetic — it never changes the exports.

studio.intro: The studio is where wraps are made: a millimeter-accurate artboard that is the vessel’s surface unrolled flat, plus a live 3D preview.
studio.altTools: Studio artboard with drawn shapes and text; the tool strip runs down the left edge.
studio.altLayers: The layers panel with three layers, one of them active.
studio.altViewer: 3D preview of a tumbler with a drawn wrap and the laser-sweep animation.

body.toolsIntro: The toolbar on the left holds nine tools. Shape tools drag out a bounding box; the pen clicks node by node (double-click or Enter to finish); freehand draws while you drag.
body.toolSelect: Click to select, move, resize and rotate elements. Double-click a path to edit its nodes.
body.toolPen: Place nodes one click at a time — double-click or press Enter to finish the path.
body.toolPolygon: Regular polygons — set the side count in the tool options bar.
body.toolStar: Stars — points and inner ratio in the tool options bar.
body.toolFreehand: Draw freehand strokes; smoothing (RDP simplification) is configurable in the tool options.
body.toolText: Click anywhere and type. Text stays live and editable — exports reference font stacks, so the laser PC needs a similar font installed.
body.toolPan: Drag the artboard around. Hold Space with any tool for temporary pan; zoom with the mouse wheel.
body.layers1: Every element lives on a layer — and layers map to assignable layers in LightBurn, so split line art, fills and photos across layers to get separate power/speed settings per pass.
body.layers2: The layers panel adds, reorders, duplicates, locks, hides and deletes layers, and sets per-layer opacity.
body.align: Select multiple elements and use the align, distribute and flip controls in the tool options bar to line them up or space them evenly — horizontally, vertically, or across the full wrap.
body.seam1: The dashed vertical lines mark the seam: the artboard’s left and right edges are the same line on the vessel. Elements crossing an edge wrap around automatically, so continuous patterns stay continuous.
body.seam2: On mugs, the handle safe zone is shown as a guide in the editor and the 3D preview — keep critical art out of it.
body.import1: The import menu in the toolbar merges an SVG file into the active layer — files are sanitized on import (scripts and external references stripped).
body.import2: PNG/JPG photos land via the Photo button and open the photo pipeline; SVGs can also be stored as library assets on the Uploads page.
body.preview: The right column renders the active vessel with your wrap as its texture — turntable rotation, a laser-sweep animation, seam and safe-zone overlays, finish materials and a custom powder-coat color.

photo.intro: The photo pipeline turns a PNG or JPG into something a laser can burn: grayscale, tone adjustments and a dither matched to your material.
photo.alt: The photo panel with an imported photo, Floyd–Steinberg dither applied, tone sliders and material preset visible.

body.import: Use the Photo button in the studio toolbar (or drop a file on the artboard). Processing is non-destructive: the pristine original stays attached to the element and Reset restores it at any time.
body.adjust: Brightness, contrast and gamma shape the midtones; grayscale, invert and sharpen prepare tricky sources. Everything re-renders live in a Web Worker — the artboard and the 3D preview update as you drag.
body.materials: Presets bundle a mode and tone curve per material — powder-coated steel, bare stainless, wood, glass and coated ceramic ship built in. Engraving convention: black pixels are where the laser fires, and invert flips that for materials that turn bright under the beam (like glass). Pick a preset as a starting point, then fine-tune.
body.ditherIntro: Lasers can’t print gray — dithering fakes it with dots. The right mode depends on the material and the look you want:
body.modeNone: None — plain grayscale; only for workflows that dither downstream.
body.modeThreshold: Threshold — a hard black/white cut at a level you set. Logos, stencils, text.
body.modeFloyd: Floyd–Steinberg — error-diffusion dither and the best-looking photos on coated metal and wood. The default for a reason.
body.modeBayer: Bayer 4×4 / 8×8 — ordered dither with a visible crosshatch texture; a stylistic choice (the wood preset uses it).
body.modeHalftone: Halftone — a classic dot grid with adjustable cell size. The choice for slate, glass and ceramic — and the only mode that converts to vector dots.
body.modeStipple: Stipple — sparse random dots, tuned by density. A delicate look on light materials.
body.halftone: Halftone mode can convert its dot grid into a layer of real vector circles — resolution-independent dots for SVG export. Keep the cell size reasonable: tens of thousands of dots get heavy, and the conversion is capped for safety.
body.bg: Remove background flood-fills from the image corners with a tolerance slider — great for solid studio backgrounds, not for complex scenes. If it eats your subject, lower the tolerance and try again.

vectorize.intro: The vectorizer traces raster images into vector paths — in your browser, in a Web Worker, with imagetracerjs.
vectorize.alt: The vectorize panel after tracing: source image selected, mono mode with threshold, result on the artboard.

body.modes: Mono traces at a single brightness threshold — the right choice for logos, stamps and silhouettes. Color quantizes to a palette (2–32 colors) for multi-tone art. Smoothing rounds the traced paths; simplification (RDP) drops redundant nodes.
body.result: The trace lands as a new layer fitted over the source image — the white background is dropped automatically. Hide or delete the raster layer afterwards.
body.when1: Vectorize flat art: logos, icons, text, anything with hard edges. Vector paths scale losslessly and engrave as crisp lines.
body.when2: Dither continuous tones: photos, gradients, shading. A traced photo becomes banded poster art; a dithered photo keeps its tonal range.

export.intro: Exports are sized in real millimeters, so your laser software imports them 1:1 — no rescaling, no guessing.
export.altSvg: The export dialog on the SVG tab with program presets and options.
export.altRotary: The export dialog’s rotary setup tab: object diameter, circumference, artboard size and step-by-step instructions.

body.svg1: Physical-size SVG (width/height in mm plus a viewBox). Pick the preset for your program:
body.programLightburn: Layers preserved as named groups — assign power and speed per layer.
body.programXtool: Layers merged into one group and shapes flattened to paths — XCS prefers a simple structure.
body.programLasergrbl: Merged, all colors forced to black — GRBL treats everything as one engrave.
body.svg2: Options: flatten shapes to paths, embed rotary metadata as a comment, and override the layer mode. The dialog shows live warnings (live text needs the font on the laser PC; rasters embed as data URIs) and a file-size estimate.
body.raster: The flat artboard rendered at 254/300/600 DPI with the DPI embedded as a pHYs chunk — programs that honor PNG resolution import at the correct physical size. The right choice when the design relies on dithered photos.
body.project: Download the project as a self-contained .laserpack (a small zip: the document, every embedded image, your uploaded 3D model and its vessel calibration, plus a thumbnail), or as a legacy .lasergen.json. Open .laserpack files from the studio toolbar, drop them on the artboard, or import them on the Library page — the browser unzips and restores everything. You can also save a copy to the library.
body.rotaryIntro: The Rotary tab computes everything your laser software’s rotary setup asks for — and the same numbers are embedded in the SVG metadata comment. For LightBurn:
body.rotary1: Note the object diameter and circumference shown in the Rotary tab (or download the .txt summary).
body.rotary2: In LightBurn: Laser Tools → Rotary Setup, enable the rotary and choose roller or chuck.
body.rotary3: Enter the object diameter — LightBurn derives steps-per-rotation from it.
body.rotary4: Import the exported SVG; it arrives at exactly the artboard size.
body.rotary5: Frame the job — tape a test band if it’s your first run on this vessel — and burn.

uploads.intro: Uploads bring your own files into the library: 3D models become vessels, images become art assets. Files never leave this device.
uploads.alt: The uploads page after calibrating an STL upload, with the saved model in the list.

body.models1: Drop a GLB or STL and the calibration form asks for the real-world max diameter (or circumference), height, engrave zone and category. The model is scaled to those millimeters — that calibration is what keeps the wrap preview and the exports accurate.
body.models2: Saving creates a library asset and a linked custom vessel, which appears in the studio’s vessel switcher under Custom. GLB models may carry multi-part materials; STL wraps as a single piece (the whole mesh takes the texture) and is assumed Z-up.
body.art: PNG/JPG uploads become photo assets and SVG uploads become sanitized svg-layer assets — insert them onto the artboard from the Library’s assets tab.
body.limits1: Files above 20 MB warn, above 50 MB are rejected — large meshes make the 3D preview sluggish.
body.limits2: Model blobs don’t travel with the library JSON export: re-upload them after restoring a backup on another device.

library.intro: The library is your local-first project shelf: every project, asset and burn job lives in IndexedDB on this device.
library.alt: The project library with saved wrap projects, thumbnails and status badges.

body.projects: Save from the studio toolbar, then search, tag, duplicate, rename and set a status. Each card shows a live-rendered thumbnail of the wrap.
body.jobs: Open a project to log burn attempts: material, power, speed, passes, notes and result photos. The tracker is where “which settings worked on the black Stanley?” gets answered once, permanently.
body.assets: Reusable art: imported SVGs, photos, AI generations and your uploaded 3D models. Insert them into any project, or send a model to the studio as your active vessel.
body.transfer: For single projects use Import project with a .laserpack file — it is fully self-contained: images, an uploaded 3D model and its vessel calibration are restored along with the document. For whole shelves, export the library as one versioned JSON file and import it on another device. Photos and SVGs travel inside the JSON; 3D model blobs do not — use .laserpack (or re-upload) for those.

help.title: Help & shortcuts
help.intro: Everything you need to design 360° wrap art: quick starts, the studio keyboard map, and answers to common questions.

gettingStarted.title: Getting started guide
gettingStarted.desc: From blank artboard to first engraving in five steps.

vessels.title: Vessels & measuring
vessels.desc: How to measure your cup and why millimeters matter.

studio.title: Open the studio
studio.desc: Jump straight into the wrap editor and 3D preview.

uploads.title: Upload models & images
uploads.desc: Bring your own GLB/STL vessels and art into your library.

export.q: Which file should I export for my laser?
export.a: Use the SVG export with the preset for your program (LightBurn, xTool Creative Space, or LaserGRBL) — it carries real millimeter dimensions. Export PNG only when your design relies on dithered photos or your workflow is raster-only.

rotary.q: How do I set up a rotary in LightBurn?
rotary.a: Open the export dialog in the studio and switch to the Rotary tab: it computes the object diameter at mid-engrave-zone and the exact artboard size. Enter those numbers in LightBurn’s rotary setup, then download the .txt summary to keep next to the machine.

stretched.q: Why does my art look stretched near the top of tapered cups?
stretched.a: That stretch is physically correct. A tapered cup’s circumference shrinks toward the narrow end, so the same millimeters of artboard width span more degrees of rotation there. The flat artboard is the exact unwrap — what you see is what burns.

data.a: Everything — projects, uploads, custom vessels, settings — lives in your browser’s IndexedDB on this device. laser-gen has no backend and no telemetry; nothing leaves your machine unless you export it yourself.

aiKeys.q: Are my AI provider keys private?
aiKeys.a: Yes. Keys are encrypted at rest (AES-GCM under a non-extractable device key), never synced, and calls go straight from your browser to the provider. There is no proxy in between.

offline.a: Yes. After the first load the app is cached by a service worker, so the studio, library, and your vessels all work without a connection. AI features are the only exception — they need the provider’s API.

more.title: Still stuck?
more.issues: Report a bug or request a feature on GitHub

uploads.intro: Bring your own 3D models (GLB/STL) and art (PNG/JPG/SVG) into your library. Files stay on this device.
uploads.dropzone: Drop a file here or click to browse
uploads.acceptHint: GLB, STL, PNG, JPG, SVG — up to 50 MB
uploads.sizeWarning: This file is larger than {max} MB — loading it in the 3D preview may be slow.

errors.unsupported: Unsupported file type. Use GLB, STL, PNG, JPG, or SVG.
errors.tooBig: File is too large — the limit is {max} MB.
errors.invalidModel: This file doesn’t look like a valid 3D model.

calibration.title: Calibrate “{name}”
calibration.hint: Enter the real-world dimensions — the model is scaled to match, so the wrap preview and export stay millimeter-accurate.

saved.model: “{name}” is ready — it’s now your active vessel in the studio.
saved.photo: Photo “{name}” saved to your library assets.
saved.svg: SVG “{name}” saved to your library assets.

uploads.emptyHint: Upload a GLB or STL above to build a custom vessel from it.
uploads.deleteConfirm: Delete “{name}”? Its linked custom vessel is removed too.
